event: Saint Jerome as Doctor of the Church
symbols: open book — Jerome's scholarly work, especially his Latin Vulgate translation of Scripture
lion at feet — the legendary lion from whose paw Jerome removed a thorn
cardinal's red robes — anachronistic attribution of cardinal rank, signifying his eminence in the Church
processional crucifix — Jerome's devotion to Christ crucified and his role as Doctor of the Church
floral background — the garden of paradise or the richness of the scriptural tradition Jerome tended
description: A bearded elderly man stands in an arched niche, wearing voluminous red cardinal's robes over a white undergarment. He holds an open book in his left hand and grasps a tall ornate processional crucifix staff in his right. A tawny lion sits at his feet on a checkered marble floor. Behind him is an elaborate backdrop of flowers and foliage against a golden ground. The figure has a halo and a contemplative expression as he reads.
theological_description: Saint Jerome (c. 347-420) is venerated as one of the four great Latin Doctors of the Church, celebrated above all for his translation of the Bible into Latin (the Vulgate), which became the standard text of Western Christendom for over a millennium. Jerome's deep knowledge of Hebrew, Greek, and Latin allowed him to produce a translation of unprecedented accuracy. He spent his later years in Bethlehem, living an ascetic life devoted to scriptural study. The lion at his feet derives from the medieval legend that Jerome removed a thorn from a lion's paw, after which the beast became his devoted companion — a story symbolizing how Christian charity tames the wildness of nature. Jerome's fierce defense of orthodox doctrine and his extensive biblical commentaries made him an indispensable authority for subsequent generations. His letters and commentaries remain primary sources for understanding early Church interpretation of Scripture.

scripture_refs.reason: All Scripture is God-breathed and profitable — the conviction that drove Jerome's life work of translation
Your word is a lamp to my feet — Jerome's devotion to making Scripture accessible
The word of our God stands forever — the permanence Jerome sought to preserve through accurate translation
